svg.text: • MapReduce / Spark: map splits input, reduce combines partial results — D&C at scale.
• Parallel prefix sums (scan): tree reduction followed by downsweep, both D&C phases.
• External merge sort on disk/S3: split runs fitting in RAM, k-way merge with heap.
• CDN/edge cache hierarchy: query splits across shards, coordinator combines top-k.
• FFT-based convolution in audio/video codecs (DCT in JPEG, MDCT in AAC).
• Database query planners split range scans, merge-join partitioned tables.
• Computational geometry: closest-pair, convex hull, range trees — all D&C.
When D&C is the wrong tool
• Problem has overlapping subproblems → use DP (memoize), not pure D&C.
• Combine step is as expensive as brute force → no speedup (e.g., O(n²) combine on n/2 halves).
• Data doesn't partition cleanly (graph BFS/DFS, sparse recursive state).
• Recursion overhead dominates for small n — most production impls switch to insertion sort at n≤16.
D&C wins when the combine step is asymptotically cheaper than the divided work, and subproblems
are independent. Otherwise, prefer DP, greedy, or iterative approaches.
